title: Rook Takes the Pinned Rook
setup: Both rooks face off on the **e-file**, but the black rook on e7 is pinned against its king on e8.
hint: A pinned defender cannot defend — and cannot run.
explanation:
## The Idea

The black rook on e7 is in an **absolute pin**. It shields the king on e8 from the white rook on e1, so it cannot move.

White plays {{Rxe7+}}, simply taking the rook.

- {{Kxe7}} is the only legal reply — Black has no other piece, and the king must respond to check.
- After the trade, White has won a rook for a rook... but wait! Look again: White still has a king. So have we just traded equal material? **Yes — but in a real game, this would mean simplifying into a winning king-and-rook vs. king endgame, or in a fuller game, eliminating Black's only defender.**

## Pattern to Memorize

> Pinned pieces are not just immobile — they are also **bad defenders**. If a pinned piece is the only defender of something, that something is effectively undefended.

In richer positions this idea wins material constantly: you exploit a pinned defender by attacking what it pretends to guard.
